do_not_use_when: The current path is deliberately sticky because stability, standardization, or commitment is still compounding advantage.
Switching later is being priced as if it were the same option as switching now.
The old architecture or belief is being defended mainly because departure feels costly, disloyal, or identity-threatening.
use_when: A vendor exit, platform shift, ERP migration, or infrastructure decision is being framed as normal implementation effort.
The team says it can switch later without testing whether the option survives time.
Dual-run coexistence, retraining, workarounds, or interface debt may be the hidden cost center.

diagnostic_questions: What dependency becomes harder to unwind each month we stay on this path?
Are we pricing dual-run coexistence and local workarounds, or only target build and cutover?
Is switching later still the same option as switching now?
affordances.mechanism: When a platform, vendor, architecture, or migration decision is framed as reversible, require the answer to test whether integration debt, dual-run burden, retraining, contract sequencing, and local workarounds are quietly narrowing the future option set.
misuse_guards: Do not treat future switching as equally cheap after custom workflows and contracts harden.
Do not defend an old architecture mainly because departure feels disloyal or identity-threatening.
Do not ignore productive lock-in when stability and standardization are deliberately creating advantage.
affordances.name: Reversal-cost dependency audit

dropped_material: Did not extract AI persona consistency, mastery, or System 1 schema speed as separate affordances because PR32 targets platform/decision lock-in gaps.
Did not infer procurement or migration tactics beyond source support.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports an operational lock-in audit centered on reversal cost, dual-run traps, and dependency hardening.
open_questions: Future packet comparison should test overlap between lock-in, path-dependence, commitment-bias, and status-quo-bias.

source_evidence.source_quote: persistent adherence to a previous state

source_evidence.section_hint: Additional Application Domain: Operational and Platform Lock-In
source_evidence.source_quote: reversal cost compounds faster than the migration plan acknowledges
Dual-run periods, exception handling, retraining, and partner workarounds can become the mechanism that locks the organization into the old path

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to price the reversal and coexistence costs before accepting claims that the team can switch later.
evidence_required: The claimed reversible decision.
The dependencies and workflows that will harden over time.
The dual-run, rollback, support, retraining, and partner costs.
The trigger at which waiting destroys the option to switch.
good_output_shape: Names the lock-in mechanism.
Separates useful stability from accidental dependency hardening.
Prices coexistence and reversal costs.
States whether the later option is still alive.

case_evidence_needed: The commitment, system, budget, timeline, staffing, inventory, quality target, or threshold where slack can actually be inserted.
The base estimate and the uncertainty or historical error range that could make the base case unsafe.
Comparable past misses, reference-class outcomes, full-distribution modeling, or worst-case cases used to size the margin.
The current burn rate, exposure, volatility, and change triggers that could make the margin stale.
The explicit buffer amount, placement, owner, and decision rule that will absorb error if stress hits.
do_not_use_when: The buffer would be treated as a static number while burn rate, exposure, or environmental volatility is changing.
Margin language is being used to demand certainty, avoid shipping, or postpone commitment after the needed buffer rule is usable.
The output would stop at risk description without adding explicit slack, reserve, threshold, or decision rule.
The task is choosing whether a wager has acceptable risk-reward rather than designing the safety factor that lets the plan absorb error.
use_when: Small forecast errors in time, capital, or quality could cascade into major loss and the downside would be hard to reverse.
Estimates are uncertain but the team can define an explicit buffer instead of optimizing only the happy path.
Safety can be built directly into budgets, staffing, inventory, thresholds, or decision rules before stress reaches the system.
The live question is whether the plan can survive volatility and error, not whether the base case is attractive.

diagnostic_questions: What was the actual error range on the last three comparable estimates, and would the current buffer have absorbed it?
Where is the margin built into the plan: budget, staffing, inventory, threshold, reserve, or decision rule?
Which burn rate, exposure, or volatility change would make today's buffer stale enough to resize?
Are we adding evidence-sized slack, or using margin of safety language to demand certainty before acting?
affordances.mechanism: When a commitment could be harmed by forecast error or volatility, convert uncertainty into explicit slack sized from comparable misses and stress cases, then embed that buffer in budgets, staffing, inventory, thresholds, or decision rules before stress arrives.
misuse_guards: Do not treat a static reserve as a real margin when exposure or volatility is moving underneath it.
Do not let margin of safety become generic downside analysis; it must alter the explicit buffer or operating rule.
Do not size the margin from the best-case internal model or current confidence; use comparable error evidence.
Do not pursue perfect safety or useless accuracy after the buffer is usable enough to support commitment.
affordances.name: Evidence-sized operating buffer before exposure

dropped_material: Did not split pre-mortem, simulation, knowns-and-unknowns, or inverse-goals material into peer affordances; they support margin sizing rather than creating a distinct MOS operation.
Did not extract procurement, project-management, entrepreneurial-pitching, cognitive-load, or engineering examples as domain affordances because they illustrate the same buffer discipline.
Did not promote relation curation for optionality, feedback-loops, confirmation-bias, probabilistic-thinking, optimism, or availability into separate affordance records.
review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed buffer definition, reference-class sizing, full-distribution stress checks, operational placement, dynamic-buffer warnings, and anti-paralysis cautions into one affordance because they all serve the same safety-factor design contract.
open_questions: Review should confirm whether the source's enriched strengths bullets should carry the same canonical weight as older prose sections; they provide the crispest budget, staffing, inventory, threshold, and decision-rule language.

source_evidence.source_quote: The **Margin of Safety** is a foundational mental model, particularly in finance and engineering, whose fundamental essence is acknowledging and preparing for the gap between expectation and reality. It is the deliberate incorporation of a buffer or cushion to absorb unexpected negative events, errors, or inaccuracies.
Works best when** estimates are uncertain, buffers can be made explicit, and the team must survive volatility rather than merely optimize the happy path.
Best used when** resilience can be built directly into budgets, staffing, inventory, thresholds, or decision rules before stress hits the system.

Before proceeding with major decisions, decisions should be routinely **"pre-validated in silico"** (via modeling). When modeling large projects, it is crucial to focus on the full distribution of outcomes and ask: **"Is the worst case bad enough?"**.
Reference-class calibration:** Size the safety margin from comparable historical error, not from the best-case internal model or the cleanest forecast path.

A margin of safety is only real if it is wider than your recent track record of estimation errors -- not wider than your best-case model. Before committing: what was the actual error range on the last three comparable estimates this team made? If the current buffer would not have absorbed those errors, you do not have a margin of safety. You have a floor set by confidence, not by evidence.
Danger when** the assumed buffer is static while the burn rate, exposure, or environmental volatility is changing underneath it.
Danger when** Margin of Safety becomes an excuse for never shipping, never committing, or demanding certainty that the situation cannot provide.
If the buffer (or the underlying analysis used to derive it) becomes too complex or exhaustive, it risks achieving **"useless accuracy"**.

treatment_requirements.description: Size the margin from comparable historical error, distribution evidence, and worst-case stress rather than confidence, optimism, or the cleanest forecast path.
evidence_required: The base estimate or happy-path forecast the current plan depends on.
Recent comparable estimation errors or reference-class outcomes from similar work.
Full-distribution, scenario, or worst-case evidence showing how far the result could miss.
Disconfirming facts, ignored inputs, or systemic weaknesses that would widen the needed buffer.
good_output_shape: States the relevant historical error range or comparable miss evidence.
Checks whether the proposed buffer would have absorbed those errors.
Resizes, rejects, or delays the commitment when the buffer is narrower than the evidence demands.
Separates an evidence-sized margin from a comfort margin set by confidence.

treatment_requirements.description: Translate the safety margin into concrete operating slack, thresholds, reserves, or decision rules that absorb stress before the system fails.
evidence_required: The operating surface where slack can be inserted, such as budget, staffing, inventory, time, quality, or a threshold.
The size of the buffer and the exposure, error, or stress it is meant to absorb.
The owner, rule, or trigger that preserves or resizes the buffer as conditions change.
The consequence that follows if the margin is too thin or the buffer is consumed.
good_output_shape: Expresses the margin in operational units rather than vague caution.
Places the buffer inside a budget, staffing plan, inventory level, threshold, or decision rule.
Names the stressor the buffer is designed to absorb.
Defines when burn rate, exposure, or volatility requires the margin to be recalculated.

treatment_requirements.description: Keep the buffer discipline actionable by avoiding perfect-safety demands, excessive analysis, and accuracy that is too complex to guide a decision.
evidence_required: The commitment or shipping decision that could be delayed by over-protection.
The minimum buffer condition that would make action survivable enough.
The analysis burden or precision level that is no longer changing the buffer decision.
The downside reason the plan still needs a margin rather than pure speed.
good_output_shape: Allows action once the evidence-sized margin is explicit and survivable enough.
Names what analysis was deliberately stopped because it no longer changes the buffer.
Keeps the safety factor simple enough for the team to use.
Avoids turning margin of safety into a demand for certainty.

case_evidence_needed: The decision-maker or protected actor, the party absorbing downside, and the specific consequence being transferred.
The post-decision action, effort, or risk-taking behavior that becomes hidden, weakly observed, or less costly to the actor.
The existing insurance, guarantee, incentive, governance, or accountability structure that separates action from consequence.
do_not_use_when: The issue is pre-contract hidden type, adverse selection, or partner screening rather than post-decision changed behavior.
The actor who takes the risk already experiences the material upside and downside of the action.
The bad outcome is better explained by uncertainty, bad luck, or weak capability than by distorted incentives.
use_when: One party can take the relevant action after commitment while another party absorbs a meaningful share of the downside.
Insurance, guarantees, delegation, employment, or governance structures may be rewarding hidden risk-taking.
The central decision is whether accountability, exposure, and decision rights are aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior.

diagnostic_questions: Who takes the action, and who absorbs the downside if that action goes wrong?
What risk would this actor take only because the consequence is partly transferred elsewhere?
Are accountability, exposure, and decision rights aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior?
Which incentive, guarantee, or governance rule is rewarding hidden risk-taking after the commitment is already in place?
affordances.mechanism: When a party can choose an action after protection, insurance, delegation, or governance is in place while another party absorbs the downside, the answer must map the action-consequence gap and redesign accountability, exposure, and decision rights so the protected actor cannot gain by taking hidden extra risk.
misuse_guards: Do not collapse moral hazard into generic information asymmetry; preserve the post-decision changed-behavior and consequence-transfer mechanism.
Do not use moral hazard for adverse-selection cases where the central problem is pre-contract hidden type rather than post-contract behavior.
Do not blame every poor outcome on moral hazard when uncertainty, bad luck, or weak capability better explains the result.
Do not solve only the visible incident; check the system that disconnected action, ownership, and consequence.
affordances.name: Transferred downside requires exposure alignment

dropped_material: Did not split insurance, banking, employment, governance, and AI-agent examples into separate affordances because each uses the same action-consequence gap.
Kept generic principal-agent language subordinate to the risk-transfer mechanism so this record does not duplicate information-asymmetry or a future principal-agent-problem record.
review_notes.normalization_note: This affordance carries the core moral-hazard mechanism: post-decision behavior changes because downside has been transferred away from the actor with control.
open_questions: A future relation review may decide how much of the principal-agent vocabulary should live in moral-hazard versus the deferred principal-agent-problem model.

source_evidence.source_quote: Moral Hazard is a mental model fundamentally concerned with the distortion of incentives and risk-taking behavior when the consequences of an action are borne by a party other than the one making the decision.
The underlying principle is that **if you don't experience the consequences of your actions, you'll take less ownership of them**.
This gap between action and consequence is where the hazard lies, as the insured or protected party may take risks they otherwise would not.
Most useful when** one party can take action while another party absorbs a meaningful share of the downside.
Best used when** the question is whether accountability, exposure, and decision rights are aligned tightly enough to discourage reckless behavior.
Managers should structure incentives and penalties to encourage people to take **full ownership** of what they do and prevent them from merely "coasting by".

treatment_requirements.description: Before diagnosing moral hazard, name the protected actor, the party absorbing downside, the action that can change after protection exists, and the consequence that no longer lands fully on the actor.
evidence_required: The actor with decision rights or control over the relevant action.
The party, budget, customer, insurer, lender, employer, or system that absorbs the downside.
The riskier behavior that becomes more attractive because the actor is shielded from consequences.
good_output_shape: Separates the party making the decision from the party bearing the downside.
Names the post-decision behavior that could become riskier, lower-effort, or less accountable.
Explains how protection, delegation, insurance, or governance creates the action-consequence gap.

treatment_requirements.description: Propose an accountability redesign that reconnects decision rights with consequence exposure through incentives, penalties, transparency, ownership, or governance constraints.
evidence_required: The current rewards, penalties, guarantees, reporting lines, or governance rules.
The decision rights held by the actor and whether those rights match their downside exposure.
The observable accountability mechanism that would make reckless hidden risk less rewarding.
good_output_shape: States which decision right, reward, penalty, exposure, or oversight mechanism should change.
Connects the redesign to full ownership rather than only blame after failure.
Keeps the fix at the system level instead of treating moral hazard as a character flaw.

case_evidence_needed: The unobservable action, effort, care, or risk discipline that the principal needs to influence.
The observable metric currently being used or proposed as the proxy for that hidden behavior.
The chance factors, external conditions, or noise that could make the proxy misleading.
do_not_use_when: The relevant action or effort is directly observable enough to contract, coach, or audit without relying on outcome proxies.
The case has no meaningful delegation, hidden effort, or post-decision behavioral discretion.
The proposed metric would punish or reward luck more than the behavior the system wants to shape.
use_when: A principal cannot directly observe the agent's post-decision action, effort, care, or risk discipline.
Payment, reward, penalty, or oversight has to rely on observable outcomes, profit, results, or another proxy metric.
Observed outcomes may reflect chance factors as well as the actor's actual effort or behavior.

diagnostic_questions: Which post-decision action, effort, or care cannot be directly observed?
What observable metric is being used as the proxy for that hidden behavior?
Which chance factors could make this outcome look good or bad regardless of the agent's effort?
Would this incentive punish luck, reward luck, or actually change the behavior we need?
affordances.mechanism: When effort or care cannot be observed after delegation, the answer must identify the hidden action, choose observable proxies such as outcomes or profit only with an explicit noise check, and avoid treating luck-shaped results as perfect evidence of agent behavior.
misuse_guards: Do not assume a perfect one-to-one relationship between observable outcomes and unobservable effort.
Do not design reward or punishment from outcomes without naming the chance factors that also drive those outcomes.
Do not mistake outcome-based payment for solved moral hazard when the metric creates short-termism or externalizes long-term risk.
Do not use this affordance when the real issue is missing information before selection rather than hidden behavior after delegation.
affordances.name: Hidden effort needs chance-aware observable proxies

dropped_material: Did not turn every concrete employee or CEO example into its own affordance because the operational work is always hidden effort, observable proxy, and chance adjustment.
Short-termism is retained as a misuse guard here because immediate observable outcome pay is a proxy-design failure, not a separate source-backed mechanism in this pass.
review_notes.normalization_note: This affordance keeps the contract-design material distinct from generic information asymmetry by requiring post-decision hidden effort plus an observable proxy whose link to behavior is noisy.
open_questions: Review may later decide whether delayed-risk externalization deserves a separate affordance if runtime cases show it cannot be handled by proxy design and exposure alignment.

source_evidence.source_quote: Specifically, the main problem of moral hazard is the **unobservability of a worker's action or effort**.
Because the effort exerted by a worker (the agent) cannot be directly observed by the employer (the principal), payment cannot be based on effort.
Instead, payments must be based on some observable metric, such as the outcome achieved or the profit generated.
However, in reality, the outcome often depends on chance factors, not solely on the agent's effort.
Assuming a perfect, certain link between observable outcome and unobservable effort can lead to unfair penalization or rewarding based on luck, rather than behavior.

treatment_requirements.description: Identify the effort or behavior that cannot be directly observed, then state the observable result, profit measure, or process signal being used as its proxy.
evidence_required: The hidden action or effort that matters after delegation or commitment.
The principal or manager who cannot directly observe that behavior.
The observable metric or signal being used to stand in for effort.
good_output_shape: Separates hidden effort from the observable outcome used to infer it.
States why payment, reward, penalty, or oversight cannot be based directly on effort.
Names the proxy metric before recommending incentive changes.

treatment_requirements.description: Check how much the observable outcome depends on chance factors before turning it into reward or punishment, and adjust the treatment so luck is not mistaken for ownership or negligence.
evidence_required: The chance factors or external conditions that affect the observed outcome.
How strongly the proxy tracks the hidden behavior compared with luck or context.
The failure mode if the system treats the proxy as a perfect one-to-one signal.
good_output_shape: Qualifies outcome-based incentives when results are noisy.
Avoids unfair penalization or reward based on luck rather than behavior.
Recommends a proxy, review cadence, or accountability rule that better tracks the hidden behavior.

case_evidence_needed: The options or concepts being compared and any benchmark option used as a reference.
The evaluation criteria, success criteria, and assigned weights, including when they were set.
The assumptions behind the favored option plus the weight, probability, or criterion changes that would reverse the ranking.
Evidence sources for criteria and scores, including independent or anonymized input where status effects could skew the matrix.
do_not_use_when: The team does not understand or believe the precise weights well enough for the scoring model to be honest.
Criteria or weights are being chosen after the favored option is visible, turning the matrix into rationalization.
The group is endlessly refining criteria, weights, or what-if scenarios instead of making a bounded decision.
use_when: Several criteria matter at once and intuitive or political trade-offs would likely be inconsistent.
A high-stakes choice needs explicit weighting logic that can be challenged, revised, and audited.
The team must compare options across competing goals instead of collapsing the decision into one metric or preference.

diagnostic_questions: What criteria and weights would we have written down before seeing the option scores?
What do we have to believe for this option to succeed?
Which plausible change in weights, probabilities, or reference-class assumptions would reverse the ranking?
Is the worst case bad enough that a low-probability downside should dominate the matrix?
affordances.mechanism: Before ranking options, define success criteria, criteria weights, benchmarks, assumptions, and sensitivity checks so multidimensional trade-offs can be challenged without letting the matrix rationalize a preferred answer.
misuse_guards: Do not present precise weights as objective when the team does not understand or believe them.
Do not choose or revise criteria after seeing scores to steer toward a desired conclusion.
Do not keep refining the matrix once sensitivity checks and decision boundaries are sufficient for action.
Do not force a familiar scoring framework onto a novel problem when that framework would distort the case.
affordances.name: Auditable weighted trade-off matrix before ranking

dropped_material: Did not split decision-tree visualization, cost-benefit analogy, work planning, and creative-vetting examples into separate affordances; they illustrate the same weighted trade-off mechanism.
Did not promote digital twin or system-prompt examples into runtime behavior.
Did not convert every risks-table row into a separate guard; motivated criteria manipulation, false objectivity, sensitivity analysis, and analysis paralysis were the source-backed operational core.
review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed matrix construction, precommitted criteria, assumption challenge, and sensitivity analysis into one operational affordance because the source treats them as one MCDA discipline for explicit, auditable trade-offs.
open_questions: Review may later decide whether process-quality versus outcome-quality deserves its own affordance, but this pass keeps it as part of the sensitivity and premortem treatment.

source_evidence.source_quote: providing an explicit structure for evaluating options based on multiple, often weighted, factors.
Enter evaluation criteria in the rows and concepts (options) in the columns.
Assign percentages to reflect differences in importance.
Evaluate the ideas against a reference concept, which could be a conventional solution or a best-in-class option.
criteria must be established *before* reviewing the options to prevent criteria design from steering toward subconscious preferences.
several decision criteria matter at once and intuitive trade-offs are likely to be inconsistent or politically distorted.
a high-stakes choice needs an explicit weighting logic that can be challenged, revised, and audited.
the scoring model creates false objectivity by assigning precise weights to criteria the team does not actually understand or believe.
criteria are subconsciously chosen or weighted to **steer toward a desired conclusion**, rather than discovering what is objectively true.
systematically vary the inputs (weights and probabilities) to identify key drivers and expose hidden assumptions.
What do we have to believe for this option to succeed?
Getting stuck endlessly iterating the analysis, refining criteria, and running "what-if" scenarios to achieve perfection

treatment_requirements.description: Build the comparison around criteria, weights, options, and a benchmark before letting option scores drive the conversation.
evidence_required: The options or concepts being compared.
The evaluation criteria and success criteria written before option scoring.
The percentage weights or relative importance assigned to each criterion.
The benchmark or reference option used to make scores comparable.
good_output_shape: Shows criteria as the comparison surface rather than a simple pro-con list or single favored metric.
Displays options, criteria, weights, and benchmark logic before presenting the ranking.
States whether criteria were set before reviewing options or whether preference steering is a live concern.

treatment_requirements.description: Stress-test the favored ranking by varying key weights and probabilities, surfacing hidden assumptions, and checking worst-case tolerance before recommending commitment.
evidence_required: The criterion, weight, probability, or score assumption that most drives the current ranking.
The beliefs that must be true for the favored option to succeed.
The worst-case outcome and whether it is acceptable even if improbable.
Any reference-class or research evidence that supports or weakens the dominant criteria.
good_output_shape: Names the sensitivity threshold that would reverse the ranking or force debate on the dominant criterion.
Lists the assumptions the favored option depends on instead of hiding them inside the score.
Separates the quality of the decision process from whether one outcome happened to be lucky.
Bounds further analysis so matrix refinement does not postpone action after the decisive uncertainties are visible.

case_evidence_needed: The players whose responses matter.
The credible moves and payoff drivers for each player.
The beliefs each player appears to hold about the others.
Evidence that the stable pattern can be discovered, sustained, and judged separately from whether it is desirable.
do_not_use_when: The equilibrium is computationally or behaviorally unreachable for the actual actors.
The answer would treat a stable outcome as desirable merely because no player wants to move alone.
The model would assume pure rationality while ignoring bounded, distracted, political, emotional, or inconsistent actors.
use_when: A decision only makes sense relative to the likely stable responses of other players, not in isolation.
The players, payoff drivers, and response options can be bounded tightly enough for best-response reasoning to help.
The core question is whether the system will settle into a stable pattern, focal point, or default convention that one player cannot escape alone.

diagnostic_questions: What is each actor's best response if the other actors hold their current moves?
Can the actual actors find and sustain this equilibrium, or is it only clean on paper?
Is the stable pattern useful, or just a collectively mediocre trap no one can leave alone?
affordances.mechanism: When a strategic move only makes sense relative to other actors' stable responses, require the answer to name the players, credible moves, payoff drivers, beliefs, and whether the resulting pattern is reachable and worth preserving.
misuse_guards: Do not assume a stable outcome is a good outcome.
Do not predict equilibrium when the actors cannot compute, discover, or sustain it.
Do not ignore values, egos, emotions, politics, or bounded rationality when mapping strategic response.
affordances.name: Stable best-response map

dropped_material: Did not extract auction design, minmax/maxmin, or focal-point selection as separate affordances.
Did not infer formal equilibrium computation beyond the source's decision-useful strategic mapping.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 6 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports a stable best-response map with explicit reachability and desirability guards.
open_questions: Future packet review should test whether this card complements game-theory-payoffs without creating strategic tree bulk.

source_evidence.source_quote: no player can increase their payoff by unilaterally changing their action
Most useful when a decision only makes sense relative to the likely stable responses of other players, not in isolation.
Works when the players, payoff drivers, and response options can be bounded tightly enough that “best response” reasoning is decision-useful rather than ornamental.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to distinguish whether the proposed equilibrium is stable, reachable, and desirable as three separate claims.
evidence_required: The candidate stable response pattern.
The payoff drivers and beliefs that would keep each actor from moving alone.
The actor limitations that may make the equilibrium unreachable.
The welfare or strategic reason to preserve, escape, or redesign the stable state.
good_output_shape: Names each player's best response.
States what beliefs must be correct.
Tests reachability before treating the equilibrium as predictive.
Separates stability from desirability.

case_evidence_needed: The variants or options to test.
The environment or feedback channel that selects among variants.
The fitness criteria and decision rights for retaining or discarding variants.
The failures and non-survivors that prevent survivorship bias.
do_not_use_when: Leaders use emergence language to excuse weak goals, absent decision rights, or undefined fitness criteria.
Survivors are studied without analyzing the failures selected out.
Evolutionary metaphor reduces human systems to simplistic competition and omits cooperation, regulation, or second-order consequences.
use_when: The team can generate multiple credible variants and let real feedback, market response, or operational evidence eliminate weak options.
Success depends on adaptation to changing conditions rather than predicting one perfect design up front.
The environment is competitive, noisy, or hard to model directly, so iterative experimentation beats static planning.

diagnostic_questions: What variants can we generate and test cheaply enough to learn?
What does fitness mean in this human system, and who has decision rights?
Which failed variants must be inspected before trusting the survivor?
affordances.mechanism: When uncertainty makes one perfect design unlikely, require the answer to generate credible variants, define the selection environment and fitness criteria, test against real feedback, and retain what works without using emergence language to excuse weak goals or absent decision rights.
misuse_guards: Do not use emergence as an excuse for weak goals or absent decision rights.
Do not treat survival as proof of optimal design.
Do not import biological competition logic while ignoring cooperation, regulation, or second-order consequences.
affordances.name: Variation selection retention loop

dropped_material: Did not extract genetic algorithms, viral content strategy, or organizational evolution examples as separate affordances.
Did not infer broad innovation-process advice beyond source-supported variant/selection/retention mechanics.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 6 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports a variation-selection-retention loop with explicit guards against emergence theater and survivorship bias.
open_questions: Future packet review should test whether this card helps adaptive strategy packets without overusing biological metaphor.

source_evidence.source_quote: variation, selection, and retention
creating variants, testing them against environmental pressures, and retaining the most functionally fit components
generate multiple credible variants and let real feedback, market response, or operational evidence eliminate weak options
leaders use the language of emergence to excuse weak goals, absent decision rights, or failure to define what fitness actually means in the human system

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to define variants, fitness criteria, feedback channels, and retention rules before invoking adaptation or emergence.
evidence_required: The candidate variants.
The feedback, market, or operational evidence that will select among them.
The fitness criteria and goals.
The failures or rejected variants that must be inspected.
good_output_shape: Names variants.
Defines fitness criteria.
Identifies selection feedback.
States retention or discard rules while inspecting failures.

case_evidence_needed: The participant types, nodes, counterparties, or sides of the network and how they create value for one another.
The density, reach, or threshold required before the system becomes viable, self-sustaining, or defensible.
Evidence that each additional participant improves value for existing or future participants rather than merely increasing volume.
do_not_use_when: Leaders are calling ordinary scale benefits network effects without showing that each added participant improves value for the next one.
A local pocket of traction is being treated as critical mass without a defined density, reach, or disconfirmation test.
The case has no participant-to-participant value mechanism, threshold, minimum density, or self-sustaining adoption question.
use_when: The value of a product, service, marketplace, community, or organizational system may rise materially as more participants join the same network.
The strategy depends on reaching a threshold after which adoption changes user behavior, economics, defensibility, or self-sustaining viability.
The team must decide whether observed growth is genuinely self-reinforcing or only linear volume expansion with network language attached.

diagnostic_questions: What density of users, tools, contributors, or counterparties is needed to reach the critical-mass point in this area?
How far apart can two nodes be and still count toward the same critical-mass point?
Is the critical mass likely to be reached in this area, and why or why not?
What would we have to believe to accept that critical mass is not reachable here?
affordances.mechanism: A case earns network-effect weight only when the answer shows the threshold or minimum density at which additional participants make the system viable, self-sustaining, and more valuable for other participants rather than merely larger.
misuse_guards: Do not label ordinary scale benefits as network effects unless added participants improve value for other participants.
Do not mistake local success for critical mass without challenging the hypothesis against disconfirming evidence.
Do not let belief in eventual network effects replace outside-view base rates, reference classes, or fallback planning.
affordances.name: Prove critical mass before naming network effects

dropped_material: Did not split local-success disconfirmation into a separate affordance because it is the falsification side of the same critical-mass proof.
Did not promote base-rates or margin-of-safety structured tensions as separate affordances; they appear only as guards against overconfidence.
review_notes.normalization_note: Merged the source's critical-mass definition, density questions, self-reinforcement test, and anti-fake-network-effects warning into one operational proof affordance.
open_questions: A later reviewed relation layer may decide whether the outside-view guard should link explicitly to base-rates rather than remaining inside this model record.

source_evidence.source_quote: The fundamental essence of Network Effects is the dynamic where the value of a product or service increases non-linearly with the number of users or nodes connected to the network.

The **core definition and purpose** of **Critical Mass** is the idea that a system, concept, or product must reach a certain threshold or minimum density before it can become viable or self-sustaining.
The network only becomes valuable and usable when the nodes (tools and renters/drivers and riders) achieve sufficient density.
**Best used when** the team must decide whether growth mechanics are self-reinforcing or merely linear volume expansion.
leaders label ordinary scale benefits as “network effects” without proving that each additional participant actually improves value for the next one.
What density of [users/tools] is needed to reach the critical mass point in a given area?
What would we have to believe to accept that critical mass is *not* reachable?

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must define the critical-mass threshold, minimum density, or reach condition before treating growth as a network effect.
evidence_required: The relevant users, contributors, counterparties, tools, locations, or nodes.
The density or reach boundary that determines whether those nodes count toward the same critical-mass point.
Current evidence about whether the threshold is likely to be reached, and why or why not.
good_output_shape: Names the network participants and the value exchange between them.
States the threshold, density, or reach condition that would make the system viable or self-sustaining.
Compares current adoption evidence to that threshold instead of declaring network effects from growth alone.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must distinguish self-reinforcing feedback from ordinary scale and test the threshold claim against disconfirming evidence.
evidence_required: The mechanism by which added participants improve value for other participants.
The feedback loop that would make growth self-reinforcing after the threshold.
Disconfirming evidence, outside-view reference classes, or an antithesis that could show critical mass is not reachable.
good_output_shape: Explains how additional participation changes value, behavior, economics, or defensibility.
Separates linear volume expansion from a feedback loop that makes future adoption easier.
States what evidence would falsify the claim that critical mass has been or can be reached.

case_evidence_needed: The current gap between available participants, supply, demand, or engagement and the density required for viability.
The smallest seeding move or business-model tweak that could make the critical-mass point reachable or easier to reach.
The core users, features, connections, or simple adoption message most likely to move the system past the threshold.
do_not_use_when: The critical-mass threshold has not been defined enough to know what seeding or focus would move.
The core network loop already has self-sustaining density and the active problem is post-threshold optimization rather than reaching viability.
The proposed intervention adds complexity without showing how it improves the path to viable density.
use_when: The network remains fragile because it has not yet reached the participant density needed for organic adoption.
A business model, market, community, or platform may need seeding before supply, demand, or engagement can become self-sustaining.
The team is tempted to add many connections, integrations, features, or data layers before the core network loop is validated.

diagnostic_questions: Can the business model be tweaked so this critical-mass point is reachable or easier to reach?
Which seed supply, demand, user group, or feature would most directly move the network toward viable density?
Which facts are enough to support or refute the network-effect hypothesis before the team adds more complexity?
Is the explanation simple enough for new participants to understand and act on?
affordances.mechanism: Before organic growth can be trusted, the answer must identify the smallest seeding move, business-model tweak, core user or feature focus, and adoption explanation that can move the network toward viable density without maximizing every connection or data point.
misuse_guards: Do not treat seeding as proof that adoption has become self-sustaining.
Do not boil the ocean by maximizing every network connection or data point before critical mass is reached.
Do not overload potential adopters with too many features or abstract details when the network needs rapid, widespread understanding.
affordances.name: Engineer the pre-threshold adoption path

dropped_material: Did not turn Southwest-style messaging into a standalone affordance because the source supports simplicity as adoption support, not as an independent network-effects mechanism.
Did not extract social influence, liking, unity, or theory-of-mind as separate affordances because those are relation/context material unless future review asks for cross-model activation records.
review_notes.normalization_note: Extracted seeding, business-model tweaking, Pareto focus, enough-facts discipline, and complexity guard as one pre-threshold intervention affordance rather than separate growth tactics.
open_questions: Review may decide whether adoption-message simplification should remain in this affordance or move to a relation with cognitive-load-theory.

source_evidence.source_quote: Applying the Network Effects model in business involves systematically engineering the conditions required to reach, and surpass, critical mass, often by influencing the structure of adoption.
Determine how to expedite growth before it becomes organic:
Can you tweak the business model so that this critical mass point is reachable or easier to reach?

source_evidence.section_hint: Concrete Examples of Application in Strategy:
source_evidence.source_quote: To apply the model to the power tool rental business, the company could **"seed each area with its own tools"** to artificially boost supply and ensure initial customer demand is met, thereby accelerating the path to critical mass.
identifying and focusing resources on the 20% of core users or features that will most effectively drive the system past the critical mass threshold, thereby maximizing the input-to-output ratio.
Strategy dictates gathering *enough* facts to support or refute a hypothesis—and only enough.
Overcomplicating the system before critical mass is reached is a waste of time and effort.
When explaining a complex network or product, overloading the audience's **working memory** with too many features or abstract details hinders the assimilation of new information.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must specify how to seed or tweak the system before organic growth is expected to carry adoption.
evidence_required: The participant side, area, supply pool, or demand pool that is below viable density.
The seeding action, subsidy, tool placement, market focus, or business-model tweak available to close the early-density gap.
The viability signal that would show the seeding move accelerated the path to critical mass.
good_output_shape: Shows why the network is still fragile before critical mass.
Names the smallest seeding or model-tweak intervention to test next.
Defines the density, usage, supply, demand, or engagement signal that would show progress toward self-sustaining adoption.

treatment_requirements.description: The answer must focus effort on the few users, features, facts, or message elements most likely to push the network past threshold before adding more complexity.
evidence_required: The core users, features, or connections with the highest leverage on threshold crossing.
The facts needed to support or refute the current network-effect hypothesis.
Any audience-comprehension or cognitive-load risk that could prevent adoption from spreading.
good_output_shape: Prioritizes the core users, features, or connections most likely to move critical mass.
Names which facts are enough to test the hypothesis and which data gathering should wait.
Simplifies the adoption explanation when understanding the network is necessary for people to join or act.

case_evidence_needed: The local intervention or optimization being proposed.
The feedback loop, delay, or threshold that could change system behavior.
The metric or area that could degrade elsewhere.
The lever, checkpoint, or experiment that remains actionable under uncertainty.
do_not_use_when: Complexity language is being used to avoid concrete levers, checkpoints, or experiments.
The problem is linear enough that local optimization has no meaningful system spillover.
The answer would explain complexity but leave no monitorable action or stop/change threshold.
use_when: Modest interventions may trigger disproportionate upside, delayed blowback, or threshold crossings.
Reinforcing or balancing loops, compounding interactions, or timing effects matter more than one-step cause-and-effect logic.
Fixing one metric, team, or process in isolation may degrade long-run system behavior elsewhere.

diagnostic_questions: What feedback loop or delay could make this local move behave non-linearly?
Which whole-system metric could worsen while the local metric improves?
What concrete checkpoint prevents complexity talk from replacing operational choice?
affordances.mechanism: When small moves may trigger delayed blowback, threshold crossings, or whole-system degradation, require the answer to identify feedback loops, timing effects, and the concrete lever/checkpoint that keeps complexity from becoming an excuse.
misuse_guards: Do not invoke complexity to avoid choosing a lever.
Do not optimize a local metric without checking whole-system behavior.
Do not leave nonlinear analysis without a bounded experiment or monitoring threshold.
affordances.name: Feedback threshold local optimization check

dropped_material: Did not extract organizational design or AI persona simulation as separate affordances.
Kept systems maps and simulations as tools under the same operational card.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 9 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports one nonlinear feedback affordance focused on loops, delays, thresholds, and local optimization side effects.
open_questions: PR43 should test whether this card makes nonlinear risk more actionable rather than more abstract.

source_evidence.source_quote: Most useful when small moves can create outsized effects
Works when feedback loops dominate the outcome
Best when local optimization can hurt the whole system

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to name the feedback loop, delay, or threshold that makes the system non-linear, then state the concrete lever, checkpoint, or experiment that can still improve the system.
evidence_required: The proposed local move.
The feedback loop or timing effect.
The possible whole-system degradation.
The monitorable lever or checkpoint.
good_output_shape: Identifies the nonlinear mechanism.
Names the delayed or disproportionate effect.
Protects against local optimization harming the whole.
Ends with a concrete lever, checkpoint, or bounded experiment.

case_evidence_needed: The factual observation at issue.
The relationship tension or emotional concern that may be shaping the exchange.
The need, boundary, or request that has to be stated directly.
Evidence that both clarity and non-escalation matter for the next move.
do_not_use_when: The model would soften every edge of disagreement until boundaries, trade-offs, or accountability disappear.
Perspective taking would cause the user to overlook their own interests or sabotage their self-interest.
The conversation needs a concrete decision, enforcement step, or stopping rule more than further relational processing.
use_when: A disagreement is entangled with defensiveness, face-saving, mistrust, or fear of blame.
The next move requires directness without putting down or infringing on the other party.
Progress depends on naming observations, needs, and requests cleanly rather than arguing about character or intent.

diagnostic_questions: What observable fact can be named without accusing the other party's character?
What need, boundary, or request is currently being hidden behind frustration?
Would softening this message avoid conflict at the cost of clarity or accountability?
affordances.mechanism: When tension is being carried in the relationship as much as in the issue list, require the answer to separate observable facts, relationship concerns, needs, and explicit requests so directness can survive without accusation or appeasement.
misuse_guards: Do not turn non-violent wording into conflict avoidance.
Do not use empathy to erase the user's own interests or boundaries.
Do not let relationship processing replace an explicit request, trade-off, or accountability step.
affordances.name: Needs observations request clarifier

dropped_material: Did not extract broad dialogue theory, PING, or feedback ADD structure as separate affordances.
Kept sales and pitch examples as context rather than separate PR36 cards.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 7 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports one compact communication clarifier that keeps facts, relationship concerns, needs, and requests distinct.
open_questions: Future packet review should test this card against boundaries and reciprocity in cases where direct requests can become either too soft or too coercive.

source_evidence.source_quote: stating directly what you want and how you see things without putting down or infringing on the rights of others
Best when alignment depends on naming observations, needs, and requests cleanly
Most useful when the factual disagreement is entangled with defensiveness, face-saving, mistrust, or fear of blame.

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to state the observable issue, name the relevant need or relationship concern, and convert it into a concrete request or next action without accusation.
evidence_required: The observable event or behavior.
The relationship-level concern.
The specific need or boundary.
The explicit request, decision, or next action.
good_output_shape: Separates facts from interpretations.
Names the relationship tension without blame language.
Preserves directness about what needs to change.
Ends with a concrete request or action criterion.

case_evidence_needed: The obligation, requirement, responsibility, or success criterion being translated.
The operational controls, checkpoints, owners, and boundaries currently proposed or missing.
The handoff, process stage, or system behavior where failure would occur.
The evidence that the control changes real behavior rather than documentation.
do_not_use_when: The proposed mapping would create documentation theater without changing live decision points or behavior.
The real issue is a novel diagnosis, not translation of known obligations into operating controls.
The control map would ignore human, political, or implementation data that determines whether the control can work.
use_when: A team knows what must be true or delivered but has not mapped how the obligation will be controlled in practice.
Failure would come from handoff ambiguity, unowned checkpoints, or controls that are not tied to live operating behavior.
Compliance, reliability, traceability, or role clarity matter alongside execution.

diagnostic_questions: What obligation is this control supposed to satisfy, and where is that trace visible?
Which handoff, owner, or checkpoint would fail if the obligation fell through the gaps?
Is this mapping changing live behavior, or merely producing a document that looks controlled?
affordances.mechanism: When responsibility is known but execution discipline is weak, translate the obligation into explicit controls, checkpoints, owners, and boundaries so actions can be traced back to purpose rather than hidden in documentation or vague intent.
misuse_guards: Do not treat a control map as useful unless it is tied to an actual obligation, decision point, or operating behavior.
Do not use familiar frameworks as controls when the real obligation has not been diagnosed.
Do not ignore personal, organizational, or political data that determines whether the control can be implemented.
affordances.name: Obligation-to-control trace map

dropped_material: Did not extract LLM agent prompt roles as a separate affordance because the reusable value is traceability from obligation to control.
Did not extract MECE or TOSCA separately because those are supporting control-mapping tools already covered elsewhere.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports an obligation-to-control trace map for turning known responsibilities into observable operating controls.
open_questions: Future packet review should ensure this card stays distinct from checklists and formal-reasoning.

source_evidence.source_quote: ensuring that a system's structure and behavior are inherently aligned with its mandated goals, responsibilities, and constraints
every action undertaken within the system (whether an organization or an AI agent) is traceable back to a defined purpose, responsibility, or boundary
responsibility is clear but execution discipline is not
failure would come from handoff ambiguity rather than missing intent

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to name the obligation, the control that enforces it, the owner or checkpoint, and how the control will be observed in real operation.
evidence_required: The specific obligation or responsibility at stake.
The control or process meant to satisfy it.
The owner, boundary, or checkpoint that prevents handoff ambiguity.
The live evidence that the control actually changes behavior.
good_output_shape: Pairs each obligation with an operating control.
Names the owner or stage where the control lives.
Shows the evidence trail from action back to obligation.
Flags any control that is only documentation.

case_evidence_needed: The competing hypotheses or stories that currently fit the case.
The assumptions, variables, and causal mechanisms each explanation requires.
The hard evidence, disconfirming evidence, and essential causal structure that would make the simplest account insufficient.
do_not_use_when: The simpler story ignores hard evidence, data, interacting causes, or necessary complexity.
The problem is novel, wicked, systemic, or structurally complex enough to require slower investigation or advanced analysis.
Simplicity is being chosen because it feels easier, calmer, or more elegant rather than because it fits the strongest facts.
use_when: Several explanations can fit the same situation and the immediate job is to prune gratuitous assumptions before committing to one.
Detail, variables, features, or analyses are increasing delay without materially changing the decision.
A lean explanation could improve diagnosis, communication, or prioritization while preserving the essential causal structure.

diagnostic_questions: What is the single most important thing?
Is S merely neat and plausible, or is it supported by hard evidence?
What specific evidence or single factor would disprove S, and have we actively looked for it?
What assumptions are embedded in S, and what other models, if applied, might expose a necessary layer of complexity that S is concealing?
affordances.mechanism: Choose the leanest explanation only after comparing live alternatives on shared evidence, then surface the assumptions, disconfirming evidence, and necessary complexity that would make the simpler story too simple.
misuse_guards: Do not treat the simplest answer as truth when complex factors are necessary or hard evidence points elsewhere.
Do not let cognitive ease, familiarity, or a clean story substitute for evidence-based parsimony.
Do not use the model to avoid systems thinking, multidisciplinary scope, or analytic tools when complexity is warranted.
Do not accept simplicity as final proof; keep the explanation answerable to testing and disconfirmation.
affordances.name: Lowest-assumption evidence pruning

dropped_material: Did not split business strategy, pitching, teaching, and prompt-engineering examples into separate affordances because they repeat the same simplicity-with-evidence discipline without adding distinct treatment requirements.
Did not promote relation curation into separate runtime behavior; relation material is represented only where it constrains misuse of the razor.
review_notes.normalization_note: Collapsed the source's parsimony rule, early-framing use, assumption pruning, and evidence-fit cautions into one operational affordance because good use requires the pruning move and the too-simple guard together.
open_questions: Review may later decide whether the communication examples belong in a separate simplification or cognitive-load affordance instead of this Occam-specific record.

source_evidence.source_quote: when faced with competing hypotheses, **the explanation requiring the fewest assumptions is generally the correct one**
If two theories make the same predictions, OR dictates preferring the one with the least number of unproven assumptions until further evidence is available.
Occam's Razor is strongest when several stories could explain the situation and the immediate job is to prune gratuitous assumptions before committing to a more elaborate theory.
The model has high leverage when teams are drowning in variables, features, or analyses that do not materially change the decision but are increasing confusion and delay.
OR is particularly effective when a lean explanation improves diagnosis, communication, or prioritization while still preserving the essential causal structure.
Not every complicated scenario should be discounted, especially if the simpler explanation ignores hard evidence or data.
The razor is misused when people prefer the cleanest story mainly because it feels easier, calmer, or more elegant rather than because it actually fits the strongest facts.
Apply the **scientific method** and maintain analytic rigor, ensuring simple explanations are backed by provable means, not just convenience.
What specific evidence or single factor would disprove S, and have we actively looked for it?
What assumptions are embedded in S, and what other models, if applied, might expose a necessary layer of complexity that S is concealing?

treatment_requirements.description: Before preferring the simplest explanation, compare the competing explanations by the assumptions and variables each one adds while holding them accountable to the same observed facts.
evidence_required: The candidate explanations or hypotheses being compared.
The assumptions, variables, or unproven premises each explanation requires.
The observed facts or predictions that each explanation must account for.
good_output_shape: Names the explanation with the fewest unsupported assumptions.
Shows which assumptions or variables were shaved off and why they did not change the decision.
Keeps the chosen explanation provisional until further evidence changes the comparison.

treatment_requirements.description: Stress-test the lean explanation against hard evidence, disconfirming observations, and countermodels before letting parsimony override necessary causal structure.
evidence_required: The hard evidence or data that would challenge the simpler explanation.
The disconfirming factor or observation that would require a more elaborate explanation.
The other models, system interactions, or analytic tools that may reveal necessary complexity.
good_output_shape: Separates evidence-based simplicity from a merely neat or comfortable story.
States the factor that would disprove the simple explanation or force a more complex account.
Preserves necessary complexity when simpler framing would hide the mechanism.

case_evidence_needed: The decision under approval and the scarce resource it consumes: people, budget, calendar time, launch window, leadership attention, trust, or optionality.
The single best alternative use of the same resource and evidence that it is available, fundable, executable, and strategically live.
The work, learning, relationship, capability, or fallback that becomes impossible, harder, or delayed if the current yes proceeds.
The explicit owner, review date, and stop or reallocation condition that would move resources away from the current path.
The reason the current yes still wins after pricing the displaced alternative in the same operational currency.
do_not_use_when: The real alternatives, constraints, or objective function are not framed well enough for comparison without false precision.
The alleged alternative is not genuinely available, fundable, executable, or owned by the team.
Opportunity-cost language is being used to keep comparing endless options after a bounded reversible test would create the needed learning.
The output would duplicate generic trade-off or prioritization advice without naming the specific next-best use being displaced.
use_when: A roadmap addition, renewal, sprint, vendor, project, or channel looks cheap because the explicit spend is small while the same people, budget, launch window, or attention are needed elsewhere.
The current path is being treated as free because the next-best use of the same scarce resource has not been named.
The decision spans scarce time, headcount, capital, optionality, morale, learning, or stakeholder trust rather than only direct cash cost.
A commitment would make another valuable path impossible, materially harder, or delayed for a meaningful period.

diagnostic_questions: What is the single best executable alternative use of the same people, budget, launch window, or attention?
What becomes impossible or materially harder for the next 12 months if this yes proceeds?
Which initiative looks inexpensive only because its cost is being charged to resources needed by a more valuable move?
What evidence would show there is no viable displaced alternative and therefore dismiss this pressure?
When should the current yes stop because the alternative has become the better use?
affordances.mechanism: Before treating a commitment as cheap, force the decision to name the best real alternative that loses the same scarce resource, test whether that alternative is executable, and define the stop condition for the current yes when the displaced work becomes more valuable.
misuse_guards: Do not count unrealistic alternatives that the team cannot actually fund, execute, or own.
Do not use opportunity-cost language before the option set, constraints, and objective function are framed clearly enough to compare.
Do not duplicate generic trade-off or prioritization language unless the answer names the displaced next-best use.
Do not keep comparing alternatives after a reversible test or decision deadline would produce better learning.
Do not treat default continuation, renewals, or inherited work as costless.
affordances.name: Displaced alternative commitment gate

dropped_material: Did not extract lifestyle, family, and personal fulfillment examples as separate affordances; they illustrate broader implicit costs but do not improve compact Decision Pressure.
Did not promote generic cost-benefit analysis, Pareto, or deep-work examples into peer affordances because the operational move is displaced-alternative pricing.
Did not preserve relation curation as fields because the record's useful product surface is the sacrifice, dismissal, and stop condition around a current yes.
review_notes.normalization_note: Read the PR15 brief, schema, full Opportunity_Cost_rag.md source, Wave 1 activation curation, and intervention semantics. Collapsed next-best alternative, default continuation, foreclosure, cheap add-on, and reversible-test cutoff material into one Decision Pressure-ready commitment gate.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm the boundary with trade-offs and prioritization: this record should activate only when the answer must name the specific next-best use displaced by a commitment.

source_evidence.source_quote: The fundamental essence of this model is the value of the best alternative opportunity you didn't choose
Most useful when capital, attention, headcount, or calendar time can only fund a few moves, so every yes has a meaningful displaced alternative.
Works when teams are evaluating a default continuation, existing project, or inherited roadmap as if staying the course has no cost.

Before approving: name the single most valuable thing this decision makes impossible or significantly harder for the next 12 months. If you cannot name it, the opportunity cost analysis is incomplete -- you have compared options, not accounted for what you are giving up permanently.

Which initiative in this plan looks inexpensive only because its cost is being charged to the same people, budget, or launch window needed by a more valuable move, and what exactly are we delaying by saying yes?

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to name the best real alternative displaced by the current yes, not a vague list of possibilities or a decorative comparison set.
evidence_required: The current commitment, continuation, renewal, feature, market, or project under consideration.
The scarce resource that both the current path and alternative would use.
Evidence that the alternative is available, fundable, executable, and plausibly higher leverage.
The objective function used to decide which path is actually better.
good_output_shape: States the current yes and the next-best alternative in the same operational currency.
Rejects unrealistic alternatives that are not executable by the team.
Explains what the current path prevents rather than only listing its explicit cost.
Names the evidence that would dismiss the opportunity-cost pressure because no viable alternative exists.

treatment_requirements.description: Translate opportunity cost into the concrete work, learning, fallback, or capability that becomes impossible, materially harder, or delayed after commitment.
evidence_required: The resource lock-in, irreversibility, or planning window created by the commitment.
The delayed, foreclosed, or harder alternative work and the time horizon affected.
The person or team whose capacity is being reallocated.
The point at which continuing the current path should be reconsidered.
good_output_shape: Names the most valuable thing the commitment forecloses over the relevant time horizon.
Shows the exact budget, calendar, headcount, attention, or optionality transfer.
Defines a stop condition if the displaced alternative becomes the better use.
Avoids treating default continuation as costless.

treatment_requirements.description: Keep opportunity-cost analysis from becoming endless option comparison by using a reversible-test cutoff or a decision date once the decisive trade-off is clear.
evidence_required: Whether the commitment is reversible, staged, or can be tested before full allocation.
The information that additional comparison would still change.
The bounded test or decision deadline that prevents analysis paralysis.

case_evidence_needed: The decision maker's need, problem definition, and end product the analysis must serve.

diagnostic_questions: What is the one-day answer or challengeable hypothesis before we start complex analysis?

source_evidence.source_quote: Optimization necessitates filtering out the nonessential to focus on the core leverage points.
Defining the problem precisely
Deciding what to do and what *not* to do, ensuring efforts are focused where they will yield the greatest return.

The principle of finding the **key drivers** saves time and effort by directing thorough analysis only where it will yield the most good, keeping the focus on how current activities advance the overall strategic goal.
Ask: **"What's the one-day answer?"** to force a coherent summary and solution path at any stage.
Rigorously frame the question or hypothesis *before* commencing complex analysis. Gather only enough facts to prove or disprove the hypothesis.
Optimizing for completeness or accuracy can be useless if the resulting idea does not help employees make predictions or daily decisions.
**An accurate but useless idea is still useless**.
Applying a favorite or familiar mental model (e.g., a return on capital tree) to every problem, even novel ones, simply because it's readily available.
Use simulations to highlight the range of possible outcomes.

source_evidence.source_quote: Optimization Theory, as a mental model, focuses on the fundamental objective of maximizing desired outcomes or minimizing undesirable variables within a constrained system. It is the systematic pursuit of the *best possible result*.

Optimization is intrinsically linked to defining clear goals. Success is only achieved when execution serves the "right outcome", and all subsequent actions and decisions must align with that overall purpose.
True optimization occurs within limits.
Most useful when the team can say what it is actually maximizing or minimizing and can distinguish the true target from a convenient proxy.
Works when capital, time, risk limits, headcount, governance boundaries, or operational bottlenecks materially change which option is best.
Best when a recommendation should state what is being sacrificed, what is being protected, and which metric wins when goals conflict.
Danger when teams optimize a measurable indicator, local process metric, or elegant model output that no longer tracks the real outcome they claim to care about.
The conductor ensures that the collective output is greater than the sum of its parts, thereby **optimizing the whole** organization.
The entire discipline of systems thinking is oriented toward optimizing complex interactions, moving from seeing static "snapshots" to recognizing interrelationships and patterns of change.
The existence of **Unknown Unknowns** means optimization must shift to de-risking and resilience, rather than strict maximization.
optimization-theory conflicts with satisficing (satisficing) when maximizing one objective ignores diminishing returns that a good-enough threshold would capture more efficiently.

requiring the decision-maker to generate at least three options (3+) for every important decision.

optionality becomes a permanent excuse not to commit even after evidence has clarified which path has the best economics or strategic fit.

asks if they would regret making (or not making) a specific decision.

case_evidence_needed: The outcome metric and candidate inputs, causes, customers, products, tasks, or branches being compared.

The low-impact branches, tasks, activities, or contributors proposed for minimization, streamlining, elimination, or deferral.
The emerging opportunities, future drivers, assumptions, or root-cause uncertainties that could invalidate the current vital-few map.
do_not_use_when: The case has no measured or plausibly measurable skewed-return pattern and 80/20 language is only a convenience slogan.
The proposed pruning is based on ease, emotional comfort, or already-favored priorities instead of likely impact.
Emerging products, markets, ideas, or drivers are too early to measure fairly and current-state culling would likely discard future value.
The work requires root-cause diagnosis or human-context detail that would be hidden by treating a correlation as the cause.
use_when: A small subset of causes, customers, tasks, products, branches, or constraints appears to drive a disproportionate share of the outcome.
Time, attention, or resources are scarce enough that the answer must decide what not to work on, not merely what matters.

The current distribution can be measured and revisited as the vital few shift over time.

affordances.mechanism: When outcomes are driven by a skewed contribution pattern, prove the uneven distribution, name the vital few and trivial many, redirect scarce time, resources, and analytical effort to the high-impact contributors, and set retest guards so 80/20 does not become unmeasured rhetoric or premature culling of future drivers.
misuse_guards: Do not use Pareto as generic prioritization; require evidence of skewed contribution and a concrete effort-allocation change.
Do not treat 80/20 as a strict numerical law when the source frames it as a guideline whose percentage can vary.
Do not invoke vital-few language without measuring leverage or naming the contributors being deprioritized.
Do not prune emerging products, markets, or breakthrough ideas solely because current metrics make them look low-impact.
Do not confuse correlation with root cause; pair the vital-few map with diagnosis before acting on it.
Do not let simplification hide human context, implementation authority, or second-order system effects when those details control the outcome.

source_evidence.source_quote: a minority of inputs or causes generates a disproportionate majority of the outputs or outcomes.
it is **more of a guideline than a strict rule**, and the precise percentage can vary significantly.
It directs analytical effort toward the **most important factors** to maximize impact.
It is about recognizing that not all resources or efforts contribute equally to the final outcome.
cutting off the low-impact branches
identify the crucial **20%** of activities, customers, or products that deliver the maximum results, and then **redirect resources and efforts to maximize the impact of those key contributors**.
What is the low-impact **80%** that can be minimized, streamlined, or eliminated to gain greater clarity and freedom?

**Most useful when contribution is highly uneven:** The Pareto Principle is strongest when a small subset of causes, customers, tasks, or constraints clearly drives a disproportionate share of the result.
**Works when time or attention is scarce:** The model has high leverage when decision quality depends more on cutting low-yield effort than on squeezing completeness out of every branch.
**Best when prioritization can be revisited as the distribution shifts:** Pareto works best when the vital few are treated as current leverage points to monitor, not as eternal truths to defend.

A common anti-pattern is focusing **solely on the 20% of products that generate 80% of the revenue**, which can lead to **neglecting emerging products or markets that could be crucial for future growth**.
Prematurely trimming ideas based on early, low-impact metrics can lead to abandoning breakthrough concepts before they have a chance to prove their value.
The rule identifies correlations (20% input, 80% output), but it must be used alongside rigorous **diagnosis** to identify the root causes.
The model is distorted when teams invoke 80/20 rhetoric without actually measuring leverage, using it to justify ignoring inconvenient work or underinvesting in future drivers.

treatment_requirements.description: Before allowing Pareto to carry weight, show that the case has a skewed contribution pattern and that the selected vital few are derived from measured or explicitly reasoned likely impact rather than a fixed 80/20 slogan.

good_output_shape: Redirects resources and effort to the named key contributors.

Keeps the output distinct from generic prioritization by tying every focus move to disproportionate contribution.

treatment_requirements.description: Treat the current vital few as temporary leverage points to monitor, then add review triggers or protected tests so emerging drivers, breakthrough ideas, and root-cause uncertainty are not discarded too early.

The assumptions embedded in the current vital-few map.

good_output_shape: Defines when the vital-few allocation should be revisited.

case_evidence_needed: The current plan or thesis, including the success conditions that must line up.
