copy practices that are merely correlational with success, rather than causal.

source_evidence.section_hint: Pre-Mortem Questions to Ensure Wise Use
source_evidence.source_quote: ensure that the adopted behavior (e.g., a celebrity's endorsement) is causally relevant to the desired outcome in the new domain, rather than just an unrelated practice of the successful person.

treatment_requirements.description: Name the proof source and test whether it is structurally similar enough to the current audience for its adoption, endorsement, quote, statistic, or behavior to deserve credibility weight.
evidence_required: Who or what is being treated as proof, including whether the source is a peer, customer, operator, statistic, quote, adopter, or high-status signal.
The current audience's relevant context, incentives, constraints, and quality-inspection limits.
The cited proof source's context, incentives, and constraints, including mismatches that would turn credibility into prestige leakage.
good_output_shape: Separates peer or customer proof from prestige-only endorsement.
States why the proof source does or does not look structurally similar to the decision-maker's situation.
Treats social proof as evidence about credibility or perceived risk, not as automatic evidence that the underlying claim is true.

treatment_requirements.description: After social proof earns limited credibility weight, require the underlying mechanism, evidence quality, customer truth, and causal transfer test before recommending adoption or copying behavior.
evidence_required: The mechanism or evidence-quality claim that would justify the decision if social proof were removed.
The customer truth, operational fact, or causal link that explains why the endorsed behavior works.
The assumptions embedded in the successful behavior being copied and the evidence that those assumptions hold in the new domain.
good_output_shape: States what social proof accelerates and what it cannot replace.
Asks whether the copied behavior transfers causally rather than copying the full bundle of visible success habits.
Names the remaining mechanism, customer evidence, or disconfirming check needed before commitment.

case_evidence_needed: The mean, base case, single estimate, or preferred scenario currently carrying the recommendation.
The quantifiable drivers, assumptions, and evidence-quality limits behind the estimate.
The plausible range of outcomes, downside tails, outliers, or one- and two-standard-deviation cases.
The success chain or failure structure that could make probability estimates too optimistic.
The assumptions that must be true for the plan to succeed and the worst-case consequence if they fail.
do_not_use_when: The only missing step is choosing and applying a historical denominator or outside-view prior; that remains base-rates work.
The parameters of uncertainty are not quantifiable enough for ranges, simulation, or scenario comparison to be honest.
The answer would compress distributional uncertainty into a summary metric that loses the inference needed for the decision.
The team is judging only whether the outcome was good or bad instead of assessing whether the thinking process was reliable.
use_when: A recommendation depends on quantifiable uncertainty, noisy evidence, or high cost of being wrong.
The current answer leans on a mean, base case, or single estimate that may hide ranges, tails, or severe outcomes.
A complex plan requires many individually likely events to all occur, or failure can happen if any component breaks.
The assumptions generating the result need to be surfaced and debated for realism before the recommendation earns confidence.

diagnostic_questions: What full range of outcomes is possible, and which tails or one- to two-standard-deviation cases would materially change the recommendation?
Is the worst case bad enough to change the decision even if the mean outcome looks acceptable?
What would we have to believe for this plan, forecast, or model output to be realistic?
Does success require a long chain of events to all go right, or can overall failure happen if any one component fails?
Are we evaluating the quality of the thinking process or merely judging the decision by the outcome that happened?
affordances.mechanism: When a recommendation depends on quantifiable uncertainty or noisy evidence, replace mean-outcome certainty with ranges, simulations, downside tails, and explicit assumption testing so the answer reflects variance, evidence quality, and failure structure rather than a heroic point estimate.
misuse_guards: Do not let a mean outcome, base case, or single heroic estimate stand in for distributional inference when tails matter.
Do not place insufficient weight on low-probability, high-impact outcomes or one- to two-standard-deviation cases.
Do not hide assumptions inside simulations, charts, or model outputs instead of spelling out what must be believed.
Do not compress statistical nuance into summary metrics that remove the distributional information needed for sound inference.
Do not judge statistical discipline by whether the outcome was good or bad; audit the decision process and evidence quality.
affordances.name: Distributional inference and downside-tail stress test

dropped_material: Did not split prediction over-optimism into a standalone affordance because the source treats it as part of range, simulation, and assumption stress testing.
Did not create a separate simplification-tension affordance; it functions as a misuse guard against losing distributional information.

Did not make base rates central here even though the source mentions them; base-rates owns denominator anchoring, while this affordance owns variance, tails, noisy evidence, and inference reliability.
review_notes.normalization_note: Merged simulation, range reporting, downside-tail attention, assumption testing, conjunctive/disjunctive probability guards, and outcome-bias protection into one distributional-inference affordance because all of them prevent mean-outcome or point-estimate overconfidence.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm the boundary with aleatory-epistemic-uncertainty-recognition: this record assumes uncertainty can be disciplined with ranges or simulations, while aleatory/epistemic should own reducible-versus-irreducible uncertainty typing.
Reviewer should confirm whether outcome-bias protection belongs in this affordance or should remain only as an absence-level guard.

source_evidence.source_quote: Instead of relying solely on a mean outcome (the base case), Statistical Discipline encourages the use of **simulations** to highlight the full range of outcomes possible.
This guards against errors like placing insufficient weight on results lying one or two standard deviations from the mean in a normal distribution.
For large projects, understanding the distribution of outcomes and the true expected case (which may be the worst case) is crucial.
Best when the recommendation should reflect ranges, downside tails, and evidence quality rather than a single heroic estimate.
The tendency to overestimate the probability that a complex plan will succeed because success requires a long series of individually likely events to all occur.
Use simulations to explore the full range of outcomes possible. Ask: **“What would you have to believe?”** for the plan to succeed, rigorously spelling out all implicit assumptions and testing them.
Ask the critical question for large projects: **“Is the worst case bad enough?”**.
where overall failure can happen if *any* component fails (a disjunctive structure), remember that probabilities of failure are often underestimated due to anchoring bias.
statistical-discipline conflicts with simplification (simplification) when statistical nuance is compressed into summary metrics that lose the distributional information needed for sound inference.
Evaluating a decision purely based on whether the result was good or bad, rather than assessing the quality of the thinking process itself.

treatment_requirements.description: When the case depends on uncertainty, require ranges, simulations, downside tails, and evidence-quality limits alongside any mean or base-case estimate.
evidence_required: The current mean outcome, base case, or point estimate.
The distribution, range, simulation, or scenario evidence available.
The low-probability, high-impact cases and one- or two-standard-deviation outcomes that could matter.
The evidence-quality limits that affect confidence in the range.
good_output_shape: Shows the range of outcomes rather than only the mean or base case.
Names the downside tail and whether the worst case could dominate the decision.
Explains how much confidence the evidence quality deserves.
Keeps the point estimate visibly subordinate to variance and tail-risk treatment.

treatment_requirements.description: Surface the assumptions required for success, test their realism, and account for conjunctive success chains or disjunctive failure structures that make intuitive probabilities unreliable.
evidence_required: The assumptions that generate the forecast, scenario, or recommendation.
The chain of events that must all succeed for the plan to work.
Any component or dependency whose failure can break the overall system.
The realism debate or test that would weaken, revise, or reject the recommendation.
good_output_shape: States what must be believed for the plan to succeed.
Identifies where conjunctive success or disjunctive failure makes probabilities easy to underestimate.
Tests assumptions for realism instead of burying them inside the model output.
Separates a good decision process from a lucky or unlucky outcome.

case_evidence_needed: The decision question, problem statement, or testable hypothesis the analysis is supposed to answer.
Whether the problem is causal, predictive, summary-statistical, simulation-oriented, or reference-class-oriented.
The simple summary statistics or heuristics that show the size and shape of the problem levers.
The assumptions, data-quality conditions, and structural-fit checks required before the chosen tool can be trusted.
The amount of evidence needed to prove or disprove the hypothesis enough to stop gathering more data.
do_not_use_when: The needed move is only to anchor a forecast to a denominator or reference-class prior; that is the distinct base-rates contract, not this record.
The decision question, hypothesis, or required output cannot yet be stated clearly enough to choose an analytical path.
A familiar framework is being forced onto a novel problem merely because the tool is available or already comfortable.
Charts, statistical language, or model outputs are being treated as proof without assumption, data-quality, and structural-fit checks.
use_when: The team is about to gather large datasets, use AI or machine learning, or build a complex model before naming the decision question.
The central information-quality issue is whether the case calls for causal analysis, prediction, summary statistics, simulation, or reference-class reasoning.
The case feels obvious to experienced operators, but noisy evidence, intuition, or high error cost could distort the conclusion.
The recommendation needs traceable logic, data, assumptions, and evidence quality rather than authority, intuition, or quantitative presentation.

diagnostic_questions: What decision question or testable hypothesis must be answered before collecting more data or building a model?
Are we trying to understand causation, predict a future state, summarize levers, simulate risk, or reason from a reference class?
What simple summary statistics show the size and shape of the problem levers before complex modeling starts?
What assumptions, data-quality checks, and structural-fit tests must hold for the chosen analytical tool to be valid?
What evidence would prove or disprove the core hypothesis enough to stop expanding the analysis?
affordances.mechanism: Before complex data gathering, modeling, forecasting, or statistical inference earns weight, frame the decision as a clear question or testable hypothesis, choose the analytical tool that fits the problem structure, and start with simple statistics that reveal the size, shape, assumptions, and stopping point of the analysis.
misuse_guards: Do not wade prematurely into data, AI, machine learning, or complex modeling before defining the question, hypothesis, and needed output.
Do not treat the most available or familiar framework as the right tool when the problem structure calls for a different analysis.
Do not let quantitative language, charts, or model outputs project rigor without checking assumptions, data quality, and structural fit.
Do not duplicate base-rates by treating reference-class anchoring as the whole affordance; here reference classes appear only as one possible analytical tool branch.
Do not keep gathering facts after the evidence is sufficient to prove, disprove, or revise the core hypothesis.
affordances.name: Hypothesis-first analytical tool fit

dropped_material: Did not split decision-tree tool choice into a separate affordance because the source uses it to enforce the same pre-analysis discipline as hypothesis-driven problem solving.

Did not turn reference-class reasoning into this affordance's center; base-rates owns reference-class and denominator anchoring, while this record only asks whether that is the right analytical branch.
Did not produce a standalone Maslow's Hammer affordance because it functions here as a misuse guard against tool misfit.
review_notes.normalization_note: Merged hypothesis-first problem framing, tool-choice discipline, simple-statistics reconnaissance, and fake-rigor guards into one affordance because they form the source's evidence-hygiene contract before statistical work earns weight.
open_questions: Reviewer should confirm the boundary with correlation-vs-causation: this affordance selects the analytical path and evidence hygiene, while correlation-vs-causation should own causal-claim validity.
Reviewer should confirm whether simple-statistics reconnaissance should remain merged with hypothesis-first tool fit rather than becoming a separate statistical-discipline affordance.

source_evidence.source_quote: Before embarking on complex analysis (like machine learning), one must rigorously frame a clear question or testable hypothesis.
This hypothesis-driven approach, used extensively in problem-solving methodologies, ensures focus and prevents wading prematurely into data without thinking through the underlying problem structure.
A core principle is the structured choice of the correct analytical tool.
Are you seeking to understand causation (drivers) or primarily seeking to predict a future state?.
Analysts should always begin by calculating simple summary statistics and heuristics to quickly see the "size and shape of your problem levers".
This reconnaissance prevents gathering huge datasets or building complicated models prematurely.
anything beyond enough facts to prove or disprove the core hypothesis is a waste of effort.
Danger when teams use quantitative language, charts, or model outputs to project certainty without checking assumptions, data quality, or structural fit.

treatment_requirements.description: Require a clear decision question, problem frame, or testable hypothesis before complex analysis begins, and keep the answer tied to what would prove or disprove that hypothesis.
evidence_required: The question or hypothesis being tested.
The decision or end product the analysis must support.
The current best one-day answer or solution path, if available.
The evidence that would prove, disprove, or force revision of the hypothesis.
good_output_shape: States the hypothesis or question before discussing data volume, model choice, or statistical technique.
Names the decision output the analysis must support.
Separates evidence that tests the hypothesis from extra facts that only add complexity.
Stops or narrows analysis when enough evidence exists to prove, disprove, or revise the hypothesis.

treatment_requirements.description: Classify the analytical job before selecting a tool, begin with simple statistics and heuristics, and justify any move to complex modeling by showing the simple pass cannot answer the decision question.
evidence_required: The analytical fork: causal drivers, prediction, summary statistics, simulation, or reference-class reasoning.
The simple statistics, heuristics, or lever sizes already checked.
The data-quality and structural-fit assumptions behind the selected tool.
The reason a more complex analysis is necessary after the simple reconnaissance.
good_output_shape: Names the analytical tool type and why it fits the problem structure.
Reports the simple-statistics reconnaissance before recommending complex modeling.
Surfaces assumptions and data-quality checks as part of statistical validity, not as optional caveats.
Rejects or defers tools whose familiar pattern does not match the current problem.

case_evidence_needed: The incumbent option and the alternative being considered.
The evidence that the status quo still performs better or worse.
The real switching costs, risks, and reversibility constraints.
The assumptions or social costs that make challenge to the default difficult.
do_not_use_when: The incumbent option is actually superior after evidence-backed comparison.
The proposed change is novelty seeking without baseline evidence that the new option is better.
The answer would treat every legacy choice as bad simply because it is old.
use_when: A current default, routine, vendor, strategy, or plan is being favored over alternatives.
The team needs to separate real switching costs from inertia, sunk costs, or unchallenged precedent.
A proposal to stay the course sounds prudent but may be disguising loss aversion or status pressure.

diagnostic_questions: Is this option surviving because it is better, or because changing feels costly?
What real switching cost exists, and what part is just inertia or precedent?
What evidence would make staying the course the correct choice rather than the comfortable choice?
affordances.mechanism: When staying the course sounds prudent, force the answer to test whether the incumbent option remains superior on evidence or is surviving because loss aversion, switching friction, default effects, or precedent make change feel costly.
misuse_guards: Do not treat every legacy choice as bad just because it is old.
Do not turn anti-status-quo energy into novelty-seeking change without baseline evidence.
Do not ignore real switching costs while accusing the team of inertia.
affordances.name: Incumbent option inertia test

dropped_material: Did not extract choice-architecture examples as separate affordances because the packet value is testing incumbent survival against alternatives.
Did not extract general System 1 cognitive economy as a separate card.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 5 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports an incumbent-option inertia test that is balanced: challenge defaults without assuming change is always better.
open_questions: Future packet review should test overlap with commitment-bias, lock-in, and path-dependence.

source_evidence.source_quote: inherent preference for the current state or default option
test whether an incumbent option is surviving because it is actually superior or merely because changing feels costly
separate real switching costs from inertia
a proposal to "stay the course" sounds prudent but may be disguising loss aversion, sunk costs, or unchallenged precedent

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to compare the current default against a live alternative, separating real switching cost from inertia before recommending staying or changing.
evidence_required: The current default or incumbent path.
A credible alternative path.
Evidence for relative performance, cost, risk, and reversibility.
The loss aversion, precedent, or social cost that may protect the default.
good_output_shape: Names the incumbent and alternative.
Separates switching cost from inertia.
States what evidence would justify staying.
States what evidence would justify changing.

case_evidence_needed: The immediate action, analysis, or detail work the team is immersed in.
The larger structure, goal, problem owner, or core point that may be obscured.
The decision quality that would improve by pausing instead of gathering more facts.
The bounded synthesis question, one-day answer, or alignment check to perform.
The next concrete move or commitment that follows after reorientation.
do_not_use_when: Reflection is being used to postpone commitment, avoid trade-offs, or keep options abstract.
The next concrete move is already clear and delay would only reduce momentum.
The proposed pause would become boiling-the-ocean research instead of bounded synthesis.
The answer would produce high-level abstraction without translating back into usable action.
use_when: The team is too close to the details to see what actually matters.
Perspective, framing, sequencing, or alignment is the bottleneck more than another marginal fact.
Acting quickly would likely lock in a shallow, biased, or poorly framed response.
A memo, decision, or workplan needs a core point, one-day answer, or alignment check before execution.

diagnostic_questions: What governing structure or core point is being obscured by the current details?
Whose point of view owns the problem, and does the current work solve that problem?
What is the one-day answer if we had to synthesize now?
Would pausing improve the decision more than gathering another marginal fact?
What concrete move follows from the reorientation, and when does the pause end?
affordances.mechanism: When immersion in details is degrading judgment, pause to identify the governing structure, core point, problem owner, and next concrete move, then return to action with a bounded frame instead of gathering another marginal fact.
misuse_guards: Do not use step-back as a socially acceptable way to avoid commitment.
Do not turn reorientation into open-ended research or perfectionist analysis.
Do not stop at abstract big-picture language without a concrete next move.
Do not gather data that does not directly prove, disprove, or support the core analysis.
affordances.name: Reorientation before execution gate

dropped_material: Did not extract learning, ghostwriting, pitching, or outside-view examples as separate cards.
Did not turn every listed framework into a field because they all serve the same reorientation-before-execution pattern.
review_notes.normalization_note: Batch 4 outcome: strong_affordance_record. The source supports a bounded reorientation gate: pause, find governing structure, synthesize the one-day answer, and return to concrete action.
open_questions: PR29 should test whether this card makes graph-only step-back useful without encouraging generic pause-and-reflect advice.

source_evidence.source_quote: necessary pause
Focusing on Structure over Detail
What’s the one-day answer?
Most useful when immediate immersion is obscuring the governing structure
Works when pausing changes the quality of the decision more than gathering another marginal fact
Danger When the Pause Becomes a Hiding Place

treatment_requirements.description: Require the answer to step back from detail immersion and name the governing structure, core point, or problem-owner frame before recommending execution.
evidence_required: The detail work, analysis, or action currently absorbing attention.
The goal, user point of view, or core question that should govern the work.
The structural frame, issue tree, or one-day answer that reorients the case.
The evidence that the current path is solving the actual problem.
good_output_shape: Names what matters more than the current detail stream.
States the core point or one-day answer in plain language.
Connects the reframe back to the problem owner's point of view.
Shows how the next move changes after the pause.

treatment_requirements.description: Keep the reflective pause bounded by requiring a next concrete move, decision, or cut-off so step-back does not become a hiding place.
evidence_required: The trade-off, commitment, or action being delayed.
The specific question the pause must answer.
The deadline, one-day answer, or minimum synthesis needed to resume action.
The risk of more research, abstraction, or perfectionism.
good_output_shape: Defines the pause as a bounded reorientation, not open-ended reflection.
Names what decision or action follows from the reframe.
Drops data gathering that does not prove, disprove, or support the core analysis.
Refuses to keep options abstract when a concrete move is now available.

case_evidence_needed: The time, money, emotional effort, identity, or other investment that is already irretrievable.
The next unit of cost, effort, exposure, or commitment being requested.
Expected future returns, marginal benefit, cash-flow reality, or comparable alternative use of resources.
Disconfirming evidence, exit criteria, or performance thresholds that would stop or change the next phase.
do_not_use_when: The exit argument ignores real remaining upside, learning value, switching costs, or residual option value.
Continuation is justified by attractive expected value after current facts and future costs and benefits are separated from past expenditure.
There is no prior-investment pressure and the live problem is ordinary option comparison or forecasting.
use_when: Prior time, money, identity, or effort is making an exit, kill, or reallocation decision feel harder than the future economics justify.
A team keeps defending continuation because of what has already been sacrificed instead of because the next step has attractive expected value.
A project, investment, partnership, or personal commitment needs a start-again-today reset from current facts.

diagnostic_questions: If we hadn't spent any money/time on this yet, would we invest in this project today based solely on the expected future returns?
What specific performance criteria must be met to justify the next phase of investment?
How would someone entirely new to this situation advise us to proceed?
What evidence exists that would fundamentally prove our original hypothesis wrong?
Is the rationale for continuing based on past effort or on rational future financial projections?
If we quit now, is the cost truly a loss, or is it freeing up resources for a greater gain?
affordances.mechanism: When prior investment, identity, or loss discomfort makes continuation feel obligatory, remove irretrievable costs from the choice, compare only marginal future value against alternatives, and recommit only if current evidence would justify starting again today.
misuse_guards: Do not use anti-sunk-cost logic as cover for quitting without evaluating learning value, switching costs, or real future upside.
Do not let ego, blind spots, or admission-of-failure framing substitute for future viability analysis.
Do not rely on book values or historical outlays when cash-flow reality is the relevant economic evidence.
Do not continue merely because prior investment is being used to justify continued exposure.
affordances.name: Future-value recommitment before continuation

dropped_material: Did not split NPV, book values, stock-sale examples, supplier switching, or venture funding into separate domain affordances because they support the same future-value recommitment operation.

Did not promote relation curation for first-principles-thinking, optionality, systems-thinking, commitment-bias, or confirmation-bias into separate affordances; those relationships clarify this record but belong in relation semantics until reviewed otherwise.
review_notes.normalization_note: Condensed the raw source and reviewed curation into one operational contract: isolate irretrievable investment, judge the next phase by future value, and guard both against escalation and careless exit.
open_questions: Future review could decide whether exit criteria should become its own affordance if runtime traces show it changes behavior independently of the recommitment test.

source_evidence.source_quote: Rational economic theory dictates that only future costs and benefits (marginal analysis) should influence a decision, yet people often "pay attention to sunk costs".
This technique focuses exclusively on the costs and benefits of the next unit of action, making the actual amount already spent irrelevant to the current decision.

Using NPV and discounted cash flow analysis focuses on the expected future value generation of the project, providing an objective financial metric that strips away emotional attachment to past outlays.
one should focus on the economic reality of **cash flows** rather than relying on accounting book values, which often include irrelevant historical costs.
The model is strongest when time, money, identity, or effort already spent is making an exit, kill, or reallocation decision feel harder than the future economics justify.
SCF has high leverage when teams keep defending continuation because of what has already been sacrificed rather than because the next step has attractive expected value.
The model becomes especially useful when decision-makers need to reset from current facts and ask whether they would willingly recommit from zero.
The model is also misapplied when people use “don’t honor sunk costs” as cover for quitting without evaluating learning value, switching costs, or the real future upside that still remains.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Proactively define specific conditions (time, budget, performance metrics) under which the commitment will be stopped or changed *before* starting the project.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Consult individuals (friends, colleagues, consultants) who lack emotional attachment or prior history with the investment.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: Rely on rigorous analytical tools like **marginal analysis** and focusing on **cash flows** to divorce the decision from emotional accounting.

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "If we hadn't spent any money/time on this yet, would we invest in this project today based solely on the expected future returns?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
source_evidence.source_quote: "Is the rationale for continuing based on past effort or on rational future financial projections?"

source_evidence.section_hint: Effective Mitigation Strategies and Pre-mortem Questions
